topic.what: Comprehensive review of probiotic evidence across major GI disorders, with specific analysis of specific bacterial and yeast strains as adjuncts to H. pylori triple therapy — showing improved eradication rates and reduced antibiotic-associated side effects.
topic.how: Narrative and semi-systematic review across conditions including IBS, IBD, C. difficile infection, antibiotic-associated diarrhea, and H. pylori gastritis; critically evaluates strain-specificity, with particular attention to Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG, Lactobacillus reuteri, and Saccharomyces boulardii in H. pylori treatment contexts. Summarizes RCT evidence and meta-analyses where available.
topic.why_matters: H. pylori eradication is the primary treatment for H. pylori-associated gastritis, but triple therapy causes significant GI side effects (diarrhea, nausea) that reduce adherence and eradication success. Adjunct probiotics that improve eradication rates while reducing side effects have direct clinical applicability and represent a microbiome-aware approach to gastritis management.

characteristics.dimension: H. pylori eradication rate improvement with probiotics
characteristics.what_it_measures: Absolute or relative improvement in eradication success when probiotics added to triple therapy
characteristics.value: 5-15% absolute improvement in eradication rates reported across trials
characteristics.vs_baseline: Standard triple therapy eradication rates ~70-80% in Western populations at time of review
characteristics.evidence: Multiple RCTs and meta-analyses cited; effect size varies by strain and regimen
characteristics.context: Antibiotic resistance patterns affect baseline eradication rates; strain choice matters significantly

characteristics.what_it_measures: Reduction in diarrhea incidence with probiotic adjunct during H. pylori therapy
characteristics.value: Significant reduction — Saccharomyces boulardii and Lactobacillus strains most evidenced
characteristics.vs_baseline: Antibiotic-associated diarrhea affects ~20-30% of H. pylori triple therapy recipients
characteristics.evidence: RCT evidence reviewed; NNT for prevention of one diarrhea episode approximately 7-10
characteristics.context: Effect well-replicated across multiple trials; S. boulardii has strongest evidence base

characteristics.what_it_measures: Degree to which review distinguishes between probiotic species and strains
characteristics.value: Strong — explicitly warns against generalizing across strains; species/strain-level evidence summarized
characteristics.vs_baseline: Earlier reviews often treated 'probiotics' as a homogeneous class
characteristics.evidence: Strain-level subgroup analysis in RCTs; mechanism-based differentiation
characteristics.context: Critical finding for clinical translation — not all probiotics are interchangeable

good_for: Clinicians adding probiotics to H. pylori triple therapy to improve outcomes
Reducing antibiotic-associated GI side effects in gastritis patients undergoing eradication
Selecting evidence-based probiotic strains (S. boulardii, L. rhamnosus GG) rather than generic products
Understanding strain-specificity as a principle for microbiome intervention design
not_for: Replacing H. pylori triple therapy with probiotics — eradication remains antibiotic-dependent
Conditions beyond GI disorders (probiotic evidence for systemic disease not reviewed)
Probiotic safety in immunocompromised patients — evidence base does not cover this population adequately
requires: Strain identification on product labels — generic 'probiotic' recommendations not clinically actionable
Updated eradication rates given rising antibiotic resistance since 2010
Awareness that probiotic market has expanded significantly; verify strains against reviewed evidence

novelty: Provided one of the first comprehensive cross-GI-disorder probiotic reviews emphasizing strain specificity over generic product effects
Synthesized H. pylori-specific probiotic adjunct evidence at a time when this was not standard of care
Established the antibiotic-associated diarrhea prevention effect as the most robustly evidenced probiotic indication in GI medicine
open_problems: Optimal probiotic strain, dose, and timing relative to antibiotic administration in H. pylori eradication are not standardized
Long-term microbiome restoration after H. pylori eradication therapy is understudied
Probiotic mechanisms of action in H. pylori inhibition (competitive exclusion, bacteriocin production, immune modulation) are not fully characterized
Whether probiotics reduce H. pylori reinfection rates post-eradication is unknown
Probiotic efficacy in antibiotic-resistant H. pylori strains has not been systematically evaluated
